habitat: Coral reefs — Indian and Pacific Oceans
description: Small, vibrantly colored fish famous for their mutualistic relationship with sea anemones, which provides shelter in exchange for the fish's waste as fertilizer.
fun_fact: All clownfish are born male; the dominant individual in a group can change sex to become female.

habitat: Open ocean — North Atlantic and Mediterranean
description: One of the largest bony fish on Earth and a powerful apex predator. Critically overfished for sushi markets, a single fish can sell for hundreds of thousands of dollars.
fun_fact: Bluefin tuna can regulate their body temperature above surrounding water, making them partially endothermic — rare among fish.

habitat: Cold rivers and northern Atlantic Ocean
description: Anadromous fish born in freshwater rivers that migrate to the ocean for feeding, then return to their birth river to spawn — often leaping waterfalls along the way.
fun_fact: Salmon can leap up to 3.65 m (12 ft) to clear waterfalls during their spawning migration.

habitat: Cold, deep waters of the North Atlantic
description: Historically one of the most economically important fish in the world; the collapse of Grand Banks cod stocks in the 1990s is a landmark case study in overfishing.
fun_fact: Cod are cannibalistic and will eat smaller members of their own species.

habitat: Tropical and temperate oceans worldwide
description: A large, highly migratory predatory fish distinguished by its elongated flat bill (rostrum), which it uses to slash through schools of prey.
fun_fact: Swordfish have a special organ that heats their brain and eyes, dramatically improving vision in cold deep water.

habitat: Warm tropical and subtropical ocean surface waters
diet: Carnivore — flying fish, squid, small pelagic fish
description: Brilliantly colored surface-dwelling fish with iridescent blue, green, and golden-yellow hues. Also called dolphinfish (unrelated to dolphins).
fun_fact: Mahi-mahi are among the fastest-growing fish; they reach sexual maturity in as little as 4–5 months.

habitat: Coral reefs — Indo-Pacific; invasive in Atlantic and Caribbean
description: Venomous reef fish with striking red, white, and brown striped pattern and elongated defensive spines. A highly destructive invasive species in the Atlantic.
fun_fact: Invasive Atlantic lionfish have no natural predators in that region, and a single female can release up to 2 million eggs per year.

habitat: Tropical and subtropical oceans, brackish and freshwater
description: Slow-moving fish that rapidly inflates its body into a spiky ball when threatened. Can consume hard-shelled prey with their beak-like fused teeth.
fun_fact: Pufferfish contain tetrodotoxin, 1,200 times more poisonous than cyanide, yet they are a prized delicacy (fugu) in Japan.

habitat: Cold, clear streams and rivers; introduced worldwide
diet: Carnivore — insects, worms, small fish, crustaceans
description: One of the most popular sport and aquaculture fish, recognizable by its distinctive pink-to-red lateral stripe and black spotting.
fun_fact: The sea-run form of rainbow trout is called a steelhead and grows significantly larger than its landlocked counterpart.

habitat: Lakes, ponds, rivers of eastern North America; introduced worldwide
description: The most popular freshwater game fish in North America, known for aggressive surface strikes and acrobatic fights when hooked.
fun_fact: Largemouth bass can detect prey using both vision and their lateral line, which senses pressure changes in the water.

habitat: Lakes and slow rivers of North America and Eurasia
diet: Carnivore — fish, frogs, waterfowl, small mammals
description: An ambush predator with a torpedo-shaped body, elongated jaws packed with sharp teeth, and superb camouflage among aquatic vegetation.
fun_fact: Pike have been documented attacking and eating ducklings, voles, and even small muskrats.

habitat: Freshwater lakes and rivers worldwide; heavily introduced
description: One of the most widely distributed freshwater fish on Earth. Disrupts aquatic ecosystems by uprooting vegetation while feeding.
fun_fact: Carp communicate using sound, producing grunts and clicks by grinding their pharyngeal (throat) teeth.

habitat: Rivers, lakes, and reservoirs of North America
diet: Omnivore — insects, crayfish, fish, plant material
description: The most numerous catfish in North America and a widely farmed food fish. Navigates murky water using chemical-sensing barbels around its mouth.
fun_fact: A 15 cm catfish has over 250,000 taste buds distributed across its entire body surface.

habitat: Lakes and rivers across Europe and northern Asia
description: A widespread freshwater fish with distinctive dark vertical stripes and reddish ventral fins. Highly adaptable and tolerant of a wide range of conditions.
fun_fact: European perch hunt cooperatively in schools, herding prey fish into shallow water before striking.

habitat: Cold, deep waters of the North Atlantic
description: The largest flatfish in the world, capable of living over 50 years and weighing more than 300 kg.
fun_fact: Halibut larvae are born swimming upright with one eye on each side; one eye migrates across the skull to the same side as they mature.

habitat: Tropical and subtropical coastal oceans worldwide
description: An elongated, powerful predatory fish with a fearsome set of large, sharp teeth. Known for curiosity toward divers but rarely aggressive.
fun_fact: Barracudas are attracted to shiny objects, which they mistake for the scales of fleeing prey fish.

habitat: Shallow tropical waters — mangroves and coral reefs
description: The largest Atlantic grouper. Once hunted to critically low levels, it has partially recovered since gaining legal protection in the US.
fun_fact: Goliath grouper produce loud booming sounds during spawning aggregations, audible to divers from significant distances.

habitat: Coral reefs of the tropical Atlantic
diet: Omnivore — sponges, algae, jellyfish, small invertebrates
description: One of the most visually striking reef fish, with a vibrant blue-and-yellow body and a distinctive iridescent blue 'crown' spot on the head.
fun_fact: Juvenile queen angelfish look completely different from adults, sporting blue stripes that mimic cleaner wrasse fish to gain access to other fish.

habitat: Western Atlantic and Gulf of Mexico around reef structures
description: A popular sport and food fish with a distinctive red body and yellow-orange eyes. Lives in social groups around hard-bottom reefs and platforms.
fun_fact: Red snapper are long-lived; the oldest verified individual was 57 years old.

habitat: Tropical and subtropical oceans worldwide
description: A fast, powerful open-ocean predator named for its bright yellow dorsal and anal fins, which lengthen dramatically with age.
fun_fact: Yellowfin tuna can sustain cruising speeds of 50 km/h and reach burst speeds exceeding 70 km/h.

habitat: Freshwater lakes, rivers, and ponds of Africa; farmed globally
diet: Herbivore / Omnivore — algae, aquatic plants, plankton
description: One of the most important aquaculture species globally, farmed on every inhabited continent as a protein source.
fun_fact: Nile tilapia are mouthbrooders — the female carries eggs and hatchlings inside her mouth for protection.

habitat: Western Atlantic and Gulf of Mexico — coastal and offshore
description: A fast, migratory predator with a streamlined silver body and deeply forked tail. Popular in sport fishing and commercial harvest.
fun_fact: King mackerel accumulate mercury through bioaccumulation; health advisories recommend limiting consumption for pregnant women.

habitat: Rivers and lakes of South America — Amazon and Orinoco basins
description: Freshwater fish with razor-sharp interlocking triangular teeth. Far less dangerous than popular culture suggests; mass feeding frenzies occur mainly on already dead or injured animals.
fun_fact: Red-bellied piranhas produce clicking sounds underwater for communication and use barking sounds when distressed.

habitat: North Pacific Ocean and rivers of Alaska, Canada, Russia
description: Named for their dramatic transformation to vivid red with a green head during spawning. Their pink flesh from krill consumption makes them commercially prized.
fun_fact: Sockeye salmon navigate thousands of miles using Earth's magnetic field and their sense of smell to locate their exact birth stream.

habitat: Tropical and subtropical ocean surface worldwide
description: Remarkable fish capable of leaping out of the water and gliding above the surface using their large, wing-like pectoral fins to escape predators.
fun_fact: Flying fish can glide over 200 m at speeds up to 70 km/h, staying airborne for up to 45 seconds.

habitat: Seagrass beds and coral reefs in tropical and temperate waters
description: Unique upright-swimming fish without scales. Uses a prehensile tail to anchor to vegetation and hunts by precise suction-feeding.
fun_fact: Male seahorses carry and give birth to the young — the only known vertebrate species where the male is solely responsible for gestation.

habitat: Tropical and temperate ocean reefs and crevices
description: Long-bodied, scaleless fish that hides in reef crevices. Their constant mouth-opening, which looks threatening, is simply how they pump water over their gills.
fun_fact: Morays have a second set of pharyngeal jaws in their throat that project forward to grab and pull prey inward — like the creature in Alien.

habitat: Mangrove swamps and streams of Southeast Asia and Australia
diet: Carnivore — insects, spiders, small lizards knocked from overhanging vegetation
description: Famous for shooting precisely aimed jets of water to knock insects off branches up to 3 m above the water surface.
fun_fact: Archerfish compensate for light refraction at the water/air boundary, adjusting their aim to accurately hit targets from below.

habitat: Shallow rice paddies and slow streams of Southeast Asia
description: Brightly colored freshwater fish with flowing fins, bred selectively for centuries. Males are highly territorial toward other males.
fun_fact: Betta fish are labyrinth fish — they breathe atmospheric air directly from the surface using a special organ, in addition to their gills.

habitat: Mangrove mudflats of Africa and the Indo-Pacific
description: Amphibious fish that spends the majority of its life on land, using modified pectoral fins as crutches to crawl, skip, and even climb mangrove roots.
fun_fact: Mudskippers absorb oxygen through their moist skin while on land (cutaneous respiration) and can survive out of water for days.

habitat: Large lakes and rivers of North America — Great Lakes, Mississippi basin
description: North America's most popular freshwater sport and table fish. Named for its large, glassy eyes packed with a reflective layer (tapetum lucidum) that gives superior low-light vision.
fun_fact: Walleye eyes glow in the dark when light hits them — the same tapetum lucidum that makes cats' eyes shine. This lets them hunt effectively at dusk and dawn when prey fish are nearly blind.

habitat: Atlantic coast of North America — spawns in freshwater rivers, lives in ocean
description: The largest member of the herring family in North America and historically the most important food fish on the East Coast. Like salmon, it migrates from the ocean back to its birth river to spawn.
fun_fact: American shad were so vital to early American survival that George Washington's troops at Valley Forge were saved in part by a massive shad run up the Schuylkill River in 1778.

habitat: Clear, cool lakes and rocky rivers of North America
description: Prized as one of the hardest-fighting freshwater sport fish pound-for-pound. Prefers cooler, clearer water than its largemouth cousin and is distinguished by vertical bronze-green bars along its sides.
fun_fact: Smallmouth bass are devoted fathers — males fan and guard the nest, then herd the school of fry for up to a month after hatching, protecting them from predators.
